problem_summary: 2014 MCM/ICM "Who is the Centennial Best Coach?" (Team 26911, marked Problem B — an evaluation/ranking problem, NOT continuous Problem A as the task metadata stated). The task: build an objective model to rank the best U.S. college sports coaches of the past century (1900-now), demonstrated across football, basketball, and baseball, then test universality on women's volleyball. The core challenge is converting a subjective "who is best" judgment into a defensible quantitative comprehensive-evaluation model while correcting for a century-long timeline bias (older coaches are systematically under-measured by internet-era popularity data).
models_used: Three-stage comprehensive evaluation framework (metrics -> subjective weights -> objective weights -> fused ranking)
Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) for subjective weights, with pairwise-comparison matrix and consistency check ('Synthesis Hierarchy Process')
Grey Relation Analysis (GRA) for objective relatedness, using an ideal reference sequence and grey relation coefficient with distinguishing coefficient rho
Grey Relation Depth Coefficient (GRDC) — the team's own novel extension of GRA (q_i(k) = normalized grey relation coefficient; D(k) = variance of GRDC)
Maximum Entropy Model (MaxEnt) formulated as a constrained nonlinear program: maximize F = -sum(w_k ln w_k) s.t. sum w_k = 1, w_k in [min q_i(k), max q_i(k)], and a variance bound from D(k)
Multiplicative weight-fusion model combining AHP (v_j) and MaxEnt&GRA (w_j) into comprehensive weight z_j = w_j*v_j / sum(w_j*v_j)
Min-max normalization of the index/decision matrix (benefit-type formula given)

Two timeline-bias correction sub-models: (a) Weighted Relative Searching Popularity (WRSP) era-ratio rescaling, and (b) a two-variable compensation using clicking-rate x retiring-year, with Google Trends curves classified into 4 trend archetypes
Google-Search-hit-count weighting of coach awards (normalized website-return counts -> award weights -> weighted sum)
Sensitivity analysis via custom 'Entropy Increase' (EI) and 'Sensitivity Index' (SI) metrics measuring ranking shift under weight perturbation
key_techniques: Subjective+objective weight fusion: deliberately pairing AHP (expert/subjective) with GRA-MaxEnt (data-driven/objective) and multiplying them, so neither pure judgment nor pure data dominates — a strong rhetorical and methodological answer to a 'subjective' problem

Quantizing the unquantifiable: turning abstract concepts (prestige, popularity) into numbers via Google Trends relative-search index and Google Search website-hit counts as a proxy for award importance

Explicit confound correction: recognizing that internet/popularity data is biased against pre-internet coaches and building dedicated WRSP era-ratio and clicking-rate x retiring-year compensations instead of ignoring the bias
Candidate pre-filtering to make the problem tractable: restrict to Division I (I-A for football), top-20 career winning percentage, minimum 10-year tenure, plus a scandal screen
Time-normalizing raw counts into ratios (awards won / awards available during tenure; championships / total championships available) to fairly compare different eras
Defining bespoke evaluation metrics (GRDC, EI, SI) and giving them formulas — signaling original contribution rather than textbook reuse
Cross-domain validation: running the identical pipeline on three sports plus a fourth (women's volleyball) to argue generalizability
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on three axes. (1) It directly confronts the hardest, most-overlooked sub-problem — timeline bias across a 100-year span — and engineers explicit, named corrections (WRSP, clicking-rate x retiring-year, ratio-normalized awards/championships) where weaker teams would silently rank modern coaches at the top and never notice. That demonstrates genuine modeling maturity. (2) The subjective+objective weight architecture (AHP fused with GRA+MaxEnt) is methodologically sophisticated and self-aware: it pre-empts the obvious judge criticism 'your weights are arbitrary' by deriving weights two independent ways and combining them, and it layers in a self-named novelty (Grey Relation Depth Coefficient) that reads as original research. (3) It is creative and resourceful with data — using Google Trends and Google Search hit-counts as measurement instruments is a memorable, non-obvious move that lets the team quantify prestige and award importance from public data. Add genuine sensitivity analysis (EI/SI) and a cross-sport + volleyball universality test producing concrete, named top-5 lists, and the judge sees a complete, honest, robust, and inventive arc from messy real-world question to defended numerical answer.
section_highlights: The Assessment Metrics section (Sec 4) is the structural backbone: it decomposes 'best coach' into three intuitive pillars — Teams' Performance, Player's Performance, Personal Prestige — each subdivided into concrete measurable indices, then mirrors that exact decomposition in the AHP hierarchy tree (Fig 3) and again in the weight-vector ordering in the Results tables. This one-to-one mapping from concept -> metric -> hierarchy -> weight -> ranking makes the whole paper legible at a glance. The Timeline Modification section (5.2.2) is the intellectual high point and the part most likely to have impressed judges, because it openly diagnoses a bias most teams miss and offers two alternative remedies. The Results section presents clean named top-5 tables per sport plus all three weight vectors (AHP / MaxEnt&GRA / comprehensive) side by side, making the fusion arithmetic transparent. The summary sheet is well written: it previews the three stages and names the key methods (AHP, MaxEnt, GRA, timeline compensation) up front. The optional 'Magazine Article' deliverable is handled with an accessible, audience-aware tone ('a thousand Hamlets in a thousand people's eyes') and reframes the model as answering 'best coach all time' vs 'best coach this time'.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Inconsistent/contradictory results reporting: the AHP weight vector is identical (0.28,0.11,0.25,0.08,0.03,0.20,0.07) across football, basketball, AND baseball tables — yet the comprehensive weights differ — which only works if AHP weights were assumed sport-independent; this is never justified and looks like a single pairwise matrix reused without re-elicitation per sport

Draftee index repeatedly collapses to weight 0 in the MaxEnt&GRA and comprehensive vectors for football and basketball, meaning a metric they argued for contributes nothing — a sign the index added noise, not signal, and was effectively dropped without discussion

The timeline correction is under-validated: the authors admit they tried and failed to fit a linear/nonlinear function ('because of the limited data, we didn't obtain an ideal outcome') and demote clicking-rate to a small-weight 'examinational standard' — so the headline timeline fix is partly aspirational

Heavy reliance on Google Trends (data only from 1994-2014) to judge century-long prestige is fundamentally mismatched to the 1900-1994 portion of the problem; the corrections mitigate but cannot create data that never existed
Google-Search-hit-count as a measure of award importance is gameable and confounded (name collisions, SEO, language) and rests on an unproven proportionality assumption stated as an assumption

Sloppy presentation: duplicated assumption bullet (the 'number of websites returned... in direct ratio' assumption appears twice), several typos/grammar slips, a garbled GRA coefficient definition ('between X_i and X_i'), and an AHP hierarchy mermaid figure cluttered with placeholder 'Policy Decisions' nodes

No ground-truth validation beyond vague appeals to 'relevant documents' / 'consistent with reality'; rankings like Tom Osborne or Cliff Gustafson over more famous names are asserted credible without an external benchmark
The EI/SI sensitivity metric is idiosyncratic and weakly defined (units, the '0.01%' arithmetic) — it gestures at robustness more than it rigorously proves it
reusable_patterns: The subjective+objective weight-fusion template: derive one weight vector by AHP (expert judgment) and a second by a data-driven method (entropy / GRA / CRITIC), then multiply-and-renormalize z_j = w_j*v_j/sum(w_j*v_j). Reusable in any MCM evaluation/ranking problem and pre-empts the 'arbitrary weights' critique

Confound-correction-first mindset: explicitly identify the systematic bias in your data (here, time/era) and build a named correction sub-model for it, rather than ranking on raw values — judges reward teams that notice the bias others miss
Convert counts to era-fair ratios: normalize achievements by what was available in that period (awards won / awards offered during tenure; titles / titles contested) before cross-era comparison

Use public web instruments (Google Trends relative index, search-hit counts) as measurement proxies for otherwise unquantifiable qualitative concepts — with an explicitly stated proportionality assumption
Tractability via principled pre-filtering: cut a huge candidate pool with a few defensible gates (top division, top-20 on a key metric, minimum tenure, integrity screen) and state them as assumptions

Concept->metric->hierarchy->weight->ranking traceability: keep the same factor ordering and grouping across the metrics section, the AHP tree figure, and every results table so the reader can follow one index end-to-end
Universality test as a built-in robustness argument: after solving the asked sports, rerun the untouched pipeline on an additional case (volleyball) to claim generalizability

Coin and formalize a small named extension (here GRDC) plus custom diagnostic metrics (EI/SI) to signal original contribution — but only if you also define them rigorously, which is where this paper was weaker
